title: Café & Restaurant
situation: You're at a Dutch café. Ordering drinks, asking about the menu, and paying.

phrases.hindi: Mag ik een koffie, alstublieft? Graag een cappuccino.
phrases.english: May I have a coffee, please? I'd like a cappuccino.
phrases.context: 'Mag ik...alstublieft?' = May I have...please? (formal). 'Graag' = please/gladly, often used at the end or alone. 'Alstublieft' (formal) vs 'alsjeblieft' (informal).
phrases.pronunciation: MACH ik uhn KO-fee, als-tu-BLEEFT? CHRAACH uhn ka-pu-CHEE-no.

phrases.hindi: Met of zonder melk? Met suiker? Welk brood wilt u?
phrases.english: With or without milk? With sugar? Which bread do you want?
phrases.context: What the staff will ask you. 'Met' = with, 'zonder' = without. 'Suiker' = sugar. Dutch cafés often ask about customization, brown vs white bread ('bruin of wit?')
phrases.pronunciation: met of ZON-der MELK? met SOW-ker? WELK BROHT WILT u?

phrases.hindi: Heeft u ook appelgebak? Is er een dagschotel?
phrases.english: Do you also have apple pie? Is there a dish of the day?
phrases.context: Apple pie ('appelgebak' or 'appeltaart') is THE Dutch café staple, always with whipped cream ('slagroom'). 'Dagschotel' = today's special. These two questions will take you far.
phrases.pronunciation: HAYFT u ook AP-pel-ge-bak? is er uhn DACH-skoh-tel?

phrases.hindi: Waar is het toilet? Is er wifi? Wat is het wachtwoord?
phrases.english: Where is the toilet? Is there wifi? What is the password?
phrases.context: Essential café questions. Dutch: 'toilet' not 'WC' in conversation. WiFi is usually free in Dutch cafés. 'Wachtwoord' = password (watch-word, guard-word).
phrases.pronunciation: WAAR is het twa-LET? is er WY-fy? wat is het WACHT-woort?

phrases.hindi: Mag ik de rekening, alstublieft? Kunnen we afrekenen?
phrases.english: May I have the bill, please? Can we pay/settle up?
phrases.context: 'De rekening' = the bill. 'Afrekenen' = to settle the bill (separable verb: 'reken af'). In Dutch cafés you often pay at the end, not per drink. Ask when you're ready to leave.
phrases.pronunciation: MACH ik de RAY-ke-ning, als-tu-BLEEFT? KUN-nen we AF-ray-ke-nen?

phrases.hindi: Kunnen we apart betalen? Ik betaal mijn deel.
phrases.english: Can we pay separately? I'll pay my share.
phrases.context: 'Apart betalen' = splitting the bill. Very Dutch, 'going Dutch' is literally Dutch! 'Mijn deel' = my part/share. Modern approach: often done with Tikkie (Dutch payment app).
phrases.pronunciation: KUN-nen we a-PART be-TAA-len? ik be-TAAL mayn DAYL.

phrases.hindi: Kan ik pinnen? Accepteert u contant?
phrases.english: Can I pay by card? Do you accept cash?
phrases.context: Most Dutch places prefer 'pinnen' (card/contactless). But some smaller cafés are cash-only. Ask beforehand! 'Contactloos' = contactless payment.
phrases.pronunciation: KAN ik PIN-nen? ak-sep-TAYRT u kon-TANT?

phrases.hindi: Het was erg lekker. Dank u voor het eten.
phrases.english: It was very tasty. Thank you for the food.
phrases.context: 'Lekker' = tasty/nice/good, the most versatile positive word in Dutch. 'Erg lekker' = very tasty. Saying this to the staff is appreciated. 'Lekker' also means nice weather, nice feeling, etc.
phrases.pronunciation: het WAS ERCH LEK-ker. DANK u VOOR het AY-ten.

grammar_notes: 'Alstublieft' (als-tu-bleeft) = formal 'please', used when receiving something too. 'Alsjeblieft' = informal. Both also mean 'here you go' when handing something over
Ordering with 'ik wil' vs 'mag ik': 'ik wil een koffie' is direct/abrupt in Dutch too. Better to say 'mag ik...' or 'ik wil graag...' or just '[item] graag'
'Graag' as a one-word answer: if someone asks 'wil je koffie?', just say 'graag' = yes please. One of the most useful words
Numbers with orders: 'twee koffie graag' = two coffees please. Dutch often omit the plural marker in spoken café orders
culture_notes: Dutch café culture: 'bruin café' (brown café) = traditional Dutch pub, usually with dark wood interior. Very cozy ('gezellig')
Coffee culture: Dutch drink a lot of coffee, often with a 'koekje' (biscuit). 'Koffie verkeerd' = coffee with lots of milk (like a latte)
Tipping is not mandatory in NL. Rounding up the bill is common. 10% is generous.
'Gezellig' is untranslatable, it means cozy/convivial/warm atmosphere. The highest compliment for a café. 'Wat gezellig hier!'

skill_breakdown.explanation: Most direct: '[item] graag' or 'mag ik [item]?'. For multiple items: 'mag ik een koffie en een appelgebak graag?' Always end with 'graag' or 'alstublieft/alsjeblieft', it softens the request.

more_examples.english: A beer please. (biertje = diminutive, very common)
Do you have anything without sugar?

practice_prompt: You are a friendly bediening at a cosy Amsterdam bruin café on a quiet weekday afternoon. The user is your customer. Default to formal 'u' (polite house style); switch to 'je' only if they open casually with 'hoi' or 'jij', and briefly note your register choice. Open verbatim with: 'Goedemiddag! Wilt u hier zitten of buiten op het terras?' Then take a drink order (nudge them toward 'mag ik een koffie?' instead of bare English), ask 'met of zonder melk?' and 'met suiker?', and offer a tosti, broodje, or 'appeltaart met slagroom?' Use 'lekker' warmly when they pick. Ring up the bill once they say 'de rekening, alstublieft' and answer 'kan ik pinnen?' (pinpas of contant, beide prima). If the user skips the 'mag ik' polite frame, uses 'het koffie' instead of 'de koffie', or orders in English without a Dutch frame, flag it as [[CORRECTION: original="..." correct="..." reason="..."]]. Keep it to 6–8 turns. End with 'smakelijk!' once the order lands, or 'tot ziens, fijne dag verder!' on exit. Stay in Dutch unless they explicitly ask to switch.
